channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: The Political Aftershocks of Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: The disasters exposed Delcy Rodríguez’s weak grasp on power.

item.title: USMCA Was Once Trump’s Brainchild. Now, It’s a Dead Deal Walking.
item.description: The White House hopes that annual reviews will curb the United States’ trade deficit with Mexico and Canada.

item.title: Bangladesh Sends Signal With China Visit
item.description: The two countries announce a “new era” in ties during Prime Minister Tarique Rahman’s trip.

item.title: What to Know About Ethiopia’s Debt Breakthrough
item.description: Addis Ababa’s debt relief process has highlighted divisions in the global financial system.

item.title: Show a liberal a Lime bike and he will show you his soul
item.description: Are the e-bikes a paragon of liberalism or an abomination?

item.title: Trans-Atlanticism Isn’t Dead—It’s Being Renegotiated
item.description: Reports of NATO’s demise have been greatly exaggerated.

item.title: Koizumi’s First Trip to Seoul: A Sign of Cautious Japan-South Korea Reconciliation
item.description: While the latest Japan-South Korea defense ministers’ meeting shows an obvious trajectory of rapprochement between the two neighbors, it also exposes certain boundaries in their defense cooperation.

item.title: Can Bending Spoons thrive as a listed company?
item.description: A rare Italian software star acts uses the private-equity playbook

item.title: Is Meloni Done With Trump?
item.description: A recent spat might be the final nail in the coffin of the trans-Atlantic far right.

item.title: Transforming the cradle of the Confederacy
item.description: Tourists are flocking to monuments on America’s racial history

item.title: Scientists have built a cell from the ground up
item.description: What do you call an organism with no evolutionary ancestors? SpudCell, apparently

item.title: Is Cuba Really Next?
item.description: The fallout from the Iran conflict and the approaching U.S. midterms may change Trump’s calculations.

item.title: China covers up a plane crash in the heart of its capital
item.description: A light aircraft flew into Beijing’s tallest building. A news blackout followed

item.title: China’s EUV Lithography Progress: Parsing Signal From Noise
item.description: China faces three specific barriers as it aims to produce key chipmaking equipment. Monitoring progress in these areas would help Western countries make better chip and AI policy.

item.title: USS Ronald Reagan Drug Case Raises Questions About Off-Base Reach in Japan
item.description: 58 sailors from the U.S. aircraft carrier were punished over an LSD distribution case that began while it was homeported in Japan. Did their network reach into Japanese society?

item.title: The Mismeasure of Europe’s Economy
item.description: While Nobel laureate economists Paul Krugman and Philippe Aghion disagree over how to measure Europe’s productivity gap with the United States, both overlook the value of its accumulated assets. That blind spot obscures the real story: Europe is living off a remarkable inheritance it has yet to convert into new growth.

item.title: Rubio Courts Tajikistan as Washington Hunts for Antimony
item.description: Restarting the bilateral dialogue after a four-year freeze is a bid to loosen China’s grip on a critically-important mineral.

item.title: Europe’s airport queues are extremely long—and getting longer
item.description: The continent’s border-control system is out of control

item.title: BrahMos Advances West: The UAE, Russia, and the Next Phase of India’s Defense Export
item.description: The BrahMos has more potential buyers than ever, but India's defense export ecosystem is still a work in progress.

item.title: Scientists can now study the event horizons of black holes
item.description: They make the universe’s most extreme gravitational laboratories

item.title: How to Spin an Empire
item.description: U.S. imperialism as inspiration and foil.

item.title: What the SpaceX IPO Tells Us About China-US Competition
item.description: Insights from Winston Ma.

item.title: NATO Needs a Defense Market
item.description: To convert NATO members' resources into military capabilities, setting defense as a strategic priority, increasing spending, and improving procurement systems are all steps in the right direction. But member countries will need to expand their industrial capacity—and that requires much more.

item.title: South Korea Gets a New PM as Ruling Party Leadership Race Heats up
item.description: The outgoing premier is expected to seek the ruling Democratic Party’s chairship amid mounting friction between the presidential office and the DP.

item.title: Is Japan a Model Middle Power?
item.description: Japan has earned rising acclaim from U.S. observers as a middle power that complements, rather than threatens, the United States. Yet Japan is not deepening its ties with Washington out of charity.

item.title: America is mighty—but becoming less dominant
item.description: Ahead of its 250th birthday, an empirical look shows the world’s leading country no longer stands so far apart

item.title: India Should Stop Panicking About Trump
item.description: The emotionalism in Indian politics obscures the strategic logic of U.S.-India ties.

item.title: Trump’s Trans-Atlantic Tech Schism
item.description: The U.S. president’s policies are driving Europe’s push for technological sovereignty.

item.title: The Digital Choices Shaping our Children’s Health
item.description: Around the world, childhood is being reprogrammed by digital technologies that shape how young people learn, play, and connect. But children and young people are not experimental subjects, a captive market, or a commodity, which is why we must shape digital environments that protect and support their healthy development.

item.title: The Middle East Has a New Saudi-Led Axis
item.description: The newfound bloc has emerged as a potential winner from the Iran war.

item.title: Pakistan, the Islamabad MoU, and the Limits of Middle Power Diplomacy
item.description: Unless the diplomatic momentum abroad translates into economic improvement at home, its impact will be limited.

item.title: Oligarchy’s Ancient Origins
item.description: The struggle between democracy and oligarchy in classical Greece was bitter, with oligarchies relying on skillfully designed political institutions to shore up unpopular regimes. As concerns grow over the “rule of the few” in the United States, Americans should consider how their own institutions are doing oligarchs’ work for them.

item.title: Blackouts Are Prabowo’s Latest Problem
item.description: Why can’t coal-rich Indonesia keep the lights on?

item.title: Worse Than an Axis
item.description: The informal alignment of U.S. adversaries is dangerous.

item.title: How to Save the U.S.-Israeli Alliance
item.description: If Iran gets a new deal with America, so must Israel.

item.title: The US Congress Should Ask What Happened in Karakalpakstan
item.description: Before granting Uzbekistan Permanent Normal Trade Relations, Washington needs to use the leverage it has to push Tashkent toward accountability.

item.title: Why Foreign Companies Are Re-assessing Their China Portfolio
item.description: From the National Intelligence Law to two new State Council decrees, China’s official messaging on foreign investment doesn’t match the actual developments on the ground.
